subject: [MSB] Make consul run as non-root
commit_message:
[MSB] Make consul run as non-root

Start whole consul container as non-root and ensure that both
dumb-init and consul are able to start and run as non-root.

Issue-ID: REQ-362
Change-Id: If5a737953122cc6cd22b8d43ac603b40f4b22727
Signed-off-by: Krzysztof Opasiak <k.opasiak@samsung.com>

hmm
consul still seen as root
Test FAIL: 5 pod(s) launched as root found
POD: onap-consul-655d9f7c4c-5w2lb container: consul uid: 0(root)
POD: onap-consul-server-0 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
POD: onap-consul-server-1 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
POD: onap-consul-server-2 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
POD: onap-msb-consul-85dd6c4d4f-cm968 container: msb-consul uid: 0(root)
https://logs.onap.org/onap-integration/gating//114698-1/security/root_pods/root_pods.log

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> hmm
> consul still seen as root
> Test FAIL: 5 pod(s) launched as root found
> POD: onap-consul-655d9f7c4c-5w2lb container: consul uid: 0(root)
> POD: onap-consul-server-0 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
> POD: onap-consul-server-1 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
> POD: onap-consul-server-2 container: consul-server uid: 0(root)
> POD: onap-msb-consul-85dd6c4d4f-cm968 container: msb-consul uid: 0(root)
> https://logs.onap.org/onap-integration/gating//114698-1/security/root_pods/root_pods.log

Hmm that's interesting. In my local kubernetes I see:
$ kubectl exec dupa-msb-consul-7bc896b6f9-7sgjh ps
PID   USER     TIME   COMMAND
    1 consul     0:56 consul agent -data-dir=/consul/data -config-dir=/consul/config -dev -client 0.0.0.0
   26 root       0:00 ps

So the only process that runs as a root is the ps that I've just executed...

Patch Set 1:

yes as far as I understand the script https://github.com/onap/integration-xtesting/blob/master/security/scripts/check_security_root.sh

we should not retrieve retrieved something like 
kubectl exec onap-msb-consul-5d44fffcdb-lfgr4 --container msb-consul -n onap id
uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root),1(bin),2(daemon),3(sys),4(adm),6(disk),10(wheel),11(floppy),20(dialout),26(tape),27(video)
what I got today
we should have uid=1(consul)

oom_redeploy

We'll Then maybe the test script is imperfect. So the entry point starts as a root but then it drops root privileges and executes main application as a root. That's how the upstream image was constructed...

The script is simple, we connect to each pod, and run id, if the id =0 / root we rise the flag.
ROOT is prohibited inside pod.

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> > Patch Set 1:
> > 
> > > Patch Set 1:
> > > 
> > > yes as far as I understand the script https://github.com/onap/integration-xtesting/blob/master/security/scripts/check_security_root.sh
> > > 
> > > we should not retrieve retrieved something like 
> > > kubectl exec onap-msb-consul-5d44fffcdb-lfgr4 --container msb-consul -n onap id
> > > uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root),1(bin),2(daemon),3(sys),4(adm),6(disk),10(wheel),11(floppy),20(dialout),26(tape),27(video)
> > > what I got today
> > > we should have uid=1(consul)
> > > 
> > > oom_redeploy
> > 
> > We'll Then maybe the test script is imperfect. So the entry point starts as a root but then it drops root privileges and executes main application as a root. That's how the upstream image was constructed...
> 
> 
> 
> The script is simple, we connect to each pod, and run id, if the id =0 / root we rise the flag.
> ROOT is prohibited inside pod.

So in my opinion what is prohibited are processes running as root not that the entrypoint starts as root which this actually checks.

So the way consul upstream container works is that it starts as a root does some prep and then drops root capabilities before starting the actual consul service (Yes, this should have been done in Dockerfile but don't blame me for that it's the way consul upstream does it).

So after this start there is no process running as a root within the container so even if the consul service is hacked it shouldn't be able to gain root privileges (unless they have LPE exploit). WHen you exec into this container you start a new process with the same privilege level as entrypoint started so effectively root and that's why this test fails even though we don't have any root process in this container unless you spawn one.

Is it an issue? It all depends on the threat model. If we want to protect our worker node from running unknown code as a root they yes. But if we assume that containers that we are running are certified and safe and we just want to protect against malicious attacker who hacked our consul service then it doesn't

Patch Set 1:

Breaking the cite line to avoid triggering redeploy once again.

> yes it is an issue, you leave the possibility to gain privilege if the root account still running.

There is no such thing as "root account still running" accounts are not running but only processes does that. That's why I wrote that if we consider consul container to be secure then there is no security issue as there is just a single process inside the container and it runs as a consul user so even if you hack it you would need LPE exploit to gain access but it looks pretty much the same if no matter if your entrypoint starts as root or not.

Think how docker works. There is a docker daemon that runs as a root. When you want to start the container this daemon executes fork(), calls unshare() to move to a separate namespace, sets up cgroups, drops priviledges and then either switch user to the one that you requested or keeps it running as root.

So the only difference in situation where docker daemon runs entrypoint as non-root and the entrypoint itself switch user are the few lines of code from the entrypoint that are executed with root permissions. So *as long as the entrypoint code is trusted* there should be no issues with that

Patch Set 3: Code-Review+1

so I think it is good
it is now an internal topic for seccom :)
consul is launched as non root but the root user still exists in the docker (discussion between Krzysztof and Fabian)
the test detects that root user is still there, that is why it reports it as still failing.
but assuming that it is launched as non root, I think it would be acceptable
